eli3: LangChain is a Python and JavaScript library that gives you pre-built building blocks for LLM applications: document loaders, text splitters, vector stores, prompt templates, chains, and agents. Instead of writing everything from scratch, you snap these blocks together.

Think of it like LEGO for LLM apps. Loading a PDF, splitting it into chunks, embedding it, storing in a vector database, and answering questions over it would take days to build manually. LangChain gives you pre-built pieces for each step that plug together in a few lines of code.

LangChain has evolved from a 'chain' model (pre-built linear sequences) to LangGraph (a graph-based model for complex agent workflows with cycles and branching). Both are maintained by LangChain Inc. and are the most widely used LLM framework as of 2024.

whenWhy.title: Rapid prototyping of RAG and agent applications
whenWhy.body: LangChain's primary value is speed to prototype: load documents with a DocumentLoader, split with a TextSplitter, embed with an Embeddings wrapper, store in a VectorStore, and answer questions with a RetrievalQA chain — all in ~20 lines of Python. The abstractions handle provider-specific API differences (OpenAI vs. Anthropic vs. Cohere) behind a unified interface. This makes it faster to switch LLM providers and faster to get a working prototype. The community ecosystem (hundreds of pre-built integrations) is a major asset.

whenWhy.title: Building stateful, multi-step agents with LangGraph
whenWhy.body: LangChain's agent framework evolved into LangGraph for production use cases. LangGraph models the agent workflow as a directed graph (with cycles, not just DAGs): nodes are steps (LLM calls, tool calls, human checkpoints), edges are transitions (conditional based on step output). This explicit graph structure enables: persistent state across nodes, human-in-the-loop at any node, parallel node execution, and time-travel debugging (rewind to any prior state). LangGraph is the recommended pattern for production agents over the older AgentExecutor.

whenWhy.title: Standardised provider-agnostic development
whenWhy.body: LangChain's provider abstraction (ChatOpenAI, ChatAnthropic, ChatGoogleGenerativeAI all expose the same .invoke() interface) allows switching LLM providers by changing one line. This is valuable for: A/B testing providers, fallback to a secondary provider on API errors, mixing providers in multi-agent systems (e.g., GPT-4 for planning, Claude for writing, Gemini for multimodal). The same abstraction applies to vector stores, embeddings, and document loaders.

deepDiveIntro: LangChain was released in October 2022 (Harrison Chase). By 2024 it was the most starred LLM framework on GitHub. The original design used 'Chains' — composable sequences of LLM calls and utilities. In 2023-2024, LangChain shifted focus to LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) for composability and LangGraph for stateful agents. LangSmith (the observability platform) is the commercial product. LangChain the framework is open source under MIT license.

Architecture: LangChain Core defines the base abstractions (Runnable protocol — the universal interface for any composable component). LangChain Community contains third-party integrations. LangChain (the main package) contains first-party implementations. LangGraph is a separate package for graph-based agents. This split allows using LangChain Core's interfaces without the full dependency tree.

deepDive.heading: LCEL: Composing Chains with the Pipe Operator
deepDive.body: LangChain Expression Language (LCEL) uses Python's | operator to compose Runnables: chain = prompt | llm | output_parser. Each step in the chain must implement the Runnable protocol (.invoke(), .stream(), .batch()). LCEL chains are automatically parallelised when steps are independent (using RunnableParallel), support streaming out of the box (the | operator forwards stream events end-to-end), and integrate with LangSmith for automatic tracing. Compared to the older class-based Chains, LCEL is more composable and easier to debug. [1]

deepDive.heading: LangGraph: State Machines for Agents
deepDive.body: LangGraph models agent workflows as state machines: (1) State — a typed dictionary updated by each node; (2) Nodes — Python functions that take the current state and return a state update; (3) Edges — transitions between nodes (unconditional: always go to next_node; conditional: go to node_A if condition else node_B); (4) Checkpointing — persist state after each node using a checkpointer (SQLite, Redis, Postgres) for resumability. Human-in-the-loop: add an interrupt_before=['tool_call_node'] to pause execution before a tool is called and wait for human approval. Time-travel: use thread_id + checkpoint_id to rewind and re-execute from any prior state. [1]

deepDive.heading: When Not to Use LangChain
deepDive.body: LangChain adds abstraction overhead that is unnecessary for simple use cases: a single LLM call that doesn't need memory, tools, or retrieval is better done directly with the provider SDK (fewer dependencies, less magic, easier debugging). LangChain critics cite: (1) frequent breaking changes (v0.1 → v0.2 → v0.3 introduced significant API churn); (2) over-abstraction making it hard to understand what's happening under the hood; (3) large dependency tree. For production systems, many teams use LangChain for prototyping and then implement the critical path directly against provider SDKs. Use LangChain when the ecosystem integrations (document loaders, vector store connectors) save significant time; use the provider SDK directly for latency-sensitive paths. [1]

takeaways: LangChain: pre-built components (loaders, splitters, retrievers, chains, agents) with a unified provider-agnostic interface. [1]
LCEL: compose Runnables with the | operator — automatic streaming, batching, and parallelisation. [1]
LangGraph: graph-based stateful agents with checkpointing, human-in-the-loop, and time-travel debugging. [1]
Provider abstraction allows switching between OpenAI / Anthropic / Google by changing one line. [1]
Avoid over-engineering simple single-LLM-call applications with LangChain — use the provider SDK directly. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you debug a complex LangChain/LangGraph pipeline when something goes wrong?
edgeCases.a: Debugging strategies: (1) LangSmith tracing — enables automatic logging of every LLM call, tool invocation, and intermediate state in a LangGraph run. Every run gets a trace URL; you can inspect inputs/outputs at each node. Set LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true and LANGCHAIN_API_KEY in environment; (2) LangGraph time-travel — if a run fails at node N, rewind to node N-1 using the checkpoint, fix the state, and re-run from that point without restarting from scratch; (3) .invoke() with config={'callbacks': [ConsoleCallbackHandler()]} — verbose logging to stdout without LangSmith; (4) Unit testing individual nodes — since LangGraph nodes are just Python functions that take state → state_update, test them in isolation. The graph structure makes it easy to mock adjacent nodes.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between LangChain Agents (AgentExecutor) and LangGraph?
edgeCases.a: AgentExecutor (older): a fixed loop — call LLM, parse output, execute tools if needed, repeat. Limited: no branching, no persistent state across restarts, no human-in-the-loop, difficult to customise the loop. LangGraph: a flexible state machine where you define the loop structure explicitly. Advantages: (1) custom control flow — add human approval nodes, retry nodes, error-handling branches; (2) persistent state — survives restarts, resumes from checkpoints; (3) multi-agent — multiple agents as nodes in the same graph, passing state between them; (4) streaming — stream graph execution events to the client in real-time. LangChain's own documentation now recommends LangGraph over AgentExecutor for all new agent development.
